title: Deep Work
content:
Deep Work is the ability to focus without distraction on a cognitively demanding task. It is the skill of producing at an elite level in terms of both quality and speed. Deep work is valuable, rare, and meaningful.

Deep work stands in contrast to shallow work — non-cognitively demanding, logistical-style tasks performed while distracted. Activities like answering emails, attending unfocused meetings, and browsing social media are examples of shallow work.

The Deep Work Hypothesis: The ability to perform deep work is becoming increasingly rare at exactly the same time it is becoming increasingly valuable in our economy. The few who cultivate this skill will thrive.

Rules for Deep Work:

1. Work Deeply. You must build rituals and routines to minimize friction when transitioning into deep work states. Decide on a depth philosophy: Monastic (eliminating all shallow work), Bimodal (dedicating some stretches to deep work), Rhythmic (daily habits of deep work), or Journalistic (fitting deep work wherever you can).

2. Embrace Boredom. The ability to concentrate is a skill you must train, not assume. Don't take breaks from distraction — take breaks from focus. Schedule specific times for internet use and resist checking outside those windows. This trains your attention to resist distraction.

3. Quit Social Media. Evaluate tools using craftsman philosophy: only use a tool if its positive impacts substantially outweigh its negatives. Social media often fails this test for knowledge workers seeking deep focus.

4. Drain the Shallows. Identify your shallow work and deliberately constrain it. Schedule every minute of your day. Quantify the depth of every activity. Ask: how long would it take to train a new hire to do this task? Less than 2 months = shallow.

Ideal deep work session: 90 minutes to 4 hours. After 4 hours of intense deep work, most people have exhausted their daily capacity for focused effort. Longer hours rarely produce proportionally better outcomes.

The 4DX Framework for Deep Work:
- Focus on the Wildly Important: identify the small number of goals that matter most
- Act on Lead Measures: track time spent in deep work, not just outcomes
- Keep a Compelling Scoreboard: a visible measure of deep work hours drives accountability
- Create a Cadence of Accountability: weekly review of your scoreboard

Shutdown ritual: At the end of each workday, review incomplete tasks, plan the next day, and say 'Shutdown complete.' This signals your brain that work is done and enables true rest — which is essential for sustained deep work capacity.
